name: Thrum Comes Up
description: Theodric has kept the bone fields register for thirty-one years. For twelve of those years, he has been in a silent collaboration with Elder Thrum of the Deep Iron Collective, exchanging names through the passage in the earth beneath the fields. He has never met her. She said, when you delivered his invitation, that she was coming. Theodric wants to know when.
accept_text: He is straightening the rows of marker-stones, which he does when he is trying to be occupied while thinking. He sets one stone precisely into its place and stands up. 'She said she was coming,' he says. 'That was two weeks ago. I don't know what two weeks means for someone who has been underground for seventy years.' He looks at the fields. 'I've been adding to the register every year for thirty-one years. The first twelve I didn't know who was leaving the names at the passage. I thought it might be a mechanism -- some old recording system the Elder People left. The lists were always accurate, always cross-referenced against my records in ways that required access to my records.' He sets another stone. 'When I understood it was a person. That someone below me was doing the same work from the other side.' He is quiet. 'I don't want to be standing here when she comes up and not know she's coming. Go down. Ask her when. Tell her I've started a second register: the Collective names we couldn't identify from surface records. I've been holding it, waiting.' He picks up the last stone. 'Twelve years I've been talking to her through paper in a hole in the ground. I'd like to know what to call her when she arrives.'
complete_text: She is at the passage entrance when you find her -- not the usual wall carving she examines, but the stone ceiling of the lowest chamber, which is made of the same material as the bone fields above. She has one hand on it, flat against the stone. She turns when you enter. 'I have been standing here,' she says, 'deciding how much of the seventy years underground matters when you're about to see the sky.' She is quiet. 'I have been underground since I was eighteen. That is a long time to be somewhere and then leave it even temporarily.' She takes her hand off the stone. 'Tell Theodric: tomorrow morning. Before the fields are warm. I would like to see the bone fields in the early light.' She pauses. 'Tell him also: I know what to call the Collective names in his second register. Forty of them I can identify from our residential records. Six of them have descendants currently living in the Collective. There are people below who have surface names they do not know belong to them.' She picks up a small leather case from the passage floor -- she has packed it already, which means she was going to come regardless of whether you came to ask. 'I have been preparing to make this trip since the morning of the Day, when I stood in the lattice and felt the surface and the deep as a single connected thing for the first time.' She looks at the stone ceiling. 'Tomorrow morning. Tell him to have tea.'
